title: City Exiles Head to the Coast and Countryside
meta: Why so many city dwellers are swapping the urban grind for the Sussex coast and countryside, and how to plan a smooth move out of town.
h1: City Exiles Head to the Coast and Countryside: The Move to Sussex
lead: Over the past few years a quiet but unmistakable trend has taken hold across the South East: city dwellers, weary of cramped flats, long commutes and pavement-grey horizons, are heading for the coast and countryside in growing numbers. Nowhere has felt this shift more keenly than Sussex, where the open Downs, the sea air and the slower pace of village life have drawn a steady flow of families and professionals out of London and the larger towns. At Wolves Removals we have moved a great many of these city exiles into their new lives near the coast, and we have learned a thing or two about what makes the leap from town to country work.
body:
For decades the gravitational pull ran the other way. People left smaller towns and villages and gravitated towards the cities, drawn by careers, culture and convenience. Something changed, though, and the change accelerated sharply after the pandemic reshaped how millions of us think about where and how we live. With remote and hybrid working now firmly established for a great many office-based roles, the daily commute no longer ties people quite so tightly to the city in which their employer happens to keep a building. Once you only need to be at your desk in person a day or two a week, the calculation about where home should be looks very different indeed.

Sussex has been one of the clearest beneficiaries of this rethink. Within easy reach of London yet wrapped in some of the loveliest scenery in southern England, the county offers a rare combination: genuine countryside, a stunning stretch of coastline, market towns full of character, and rail links that still make an occasional office day perfectly manageable. We have helped families settle everywhere from the villages around the South Downs to the seafront towns along the Channel, and the story behind each move is remarkably consistent. People want more space, more greenery, more time, and a stronger sense of community than the city was giving them.

What Is Driving the Move Out of the Cities?

No single factor explains the trend. Rather, a cluster of pressures and pulls have combined to make the coast and countryside feel not just appealing but achievable for people who would never previously have considered leaving the city. Understanding what is behind the shift helps if you are weighing up a move of your own.

Space and Value for Money

The most obvious driver is space. A budget that buys a small flat in a city often stretches to a house with a garden once you move out of town. For growing families in particular, the prospect of a spare bedroom, a home office, somewhere for the children to play and a bit of breathing room is enormously attractive. The countryside and coastal towns of Sussex offer that extra space in a way that dense urban areas simply cannot, and the value can feel transformative when you have been squeezed into a flat for years.

The Rise of Flexible Working

Hybrid working has been the great enabler. When the office is somewhere you visit rather than somewhere you go every day, the geography of your life opens up. A spare bedroom becomes a study, the commute becomes occasional, and suddenly living an hour from the city by train feels entirely reasonable. We have moved a striking number of people whose decision came down to one simple realisation: they no longer needed to live where they worked.

Wellbeing and Quality of Life

Beyond the practical lies something less tangible but no less powerful. People talk to us about wanting to wake up to birdsong rather than traffic, to walk on the beach before work, to know their neighbours, and to give their children the kind of outdoor childhood that is harder to come by in the inner city. The South Downs National Park, the beaches stretching along the coast, and the network of footpaths and bridleways that thread through the county all feed a real appetite for a healthier, calmer way of living.

Where Are People Moving To?

Sussex is wonderfully varied, and the right destination depends entirely on what you are looking for. Some buyers are drawn to the energy and culture of the larger coastal towns, while others want the deep quiet of a Downland village. Here is a flavour of the kinds of places we move people to, and what each offers.

For those who want the sea on their doorstep, the coastal towns are an obvious draw. We carry out a great many removals in Worthing, a town that blends a relaxed seaside feel with proper amenities, good schools and a genuine sense of community. Further east, the city by the sea continues to attract creative professionals and young families alike, and our Brighton removals service has been busy with people trading a London postcode for life beside the Channel.

Inland, the appeal is different but every bit as strong. The market towns and villages around the Downs offer countryside on the doorstep with the convenience of shops, pubs and stations close at hand. We are based near Pulborough ourselves, so we know the villages of the Arun valley intimately and handle plenty of moves in the surrounding area, from Storrington at the foot of the Downs to the busy commuter hub of Horsham with its excellent rail links into the capital. To the west, the cathedral city and its surrounding villages remain perennially popular, and our Chichester removals team knows the area well.

If you are still deciding, it can help to think in broader strokes about the character of the county before narrowing down to a particular town. Our overview of removals across West Sussex sets out the lie of the land, from the coastal plain to the wooded Weald, and gives a useful sense of which corners of the county might suit you best.

The Realities of Country and Coastal Living

It would be dishonest to pretend that leaving the city is all gain and no compromise, and as a firm that wants you to be genuinely happy in your new home, we would rather be straight with you. Country and coastal life is wonderful, but it does come with adjustments that catch some newcomers off guard.

Rural transport is thinner than in the city. Buses are less frequent, and a car often becomes a necessity rather than a convenience, particularly in the smaller villages. Broadband, while much improved, can still be patchy in the more remote pockets, which matters a great deal if you are relying on it for work; it is always worth checking the connection at a specific property before you commit. Amenities are more spread out, so the corner shop and the late-night supermarket of city life give way to a weekly trip into town. And coastal properties bring their own considerations, from salt-laden air that is harder on paintwork and metal to the need to think carefully about exposure and weather.

None of this should put you off. The overwhelming majority of the people we move tell us, months later, that they only wish they had done it sooner. The point is simply to go in with your eyes open, choosing the location that genuinely fits the life you want to lead rather than an idealised picture of country living.

Planning a Move Out of the City

A move from the city to the coast or countryside is often a longer-distance move than people are used to, and that brings a few extra considerations. The logistics tend to be more involved, access at the country end can be trickier, and the emotional weight of a bigger life change adds its own pressure. A little forethought goes a long way.

Start Early and Declutter

Moving is the perfect prompt to take stock of what you actually want to carry into your new life. Many city movers have accumulated possessions to fill years in a flat, only to find that some of it no longer suits a different kind of home. Decluttering before you pack saves money, effort and space, and it makes the unpacking at the other end far less daunting. If you find yourself with things you want to keep but no immediate room for, our secure storage solutions can bridge the gap while you settle in and work out where everything belongs.

Think About Access at Both Ends

City moves often involve parking restrictions, narrow streets and flights of stairs, while the country end may bring long, unmade lanes, low bridges, tight gateways or properties that a large lorry simply cannot reach. Telling your removals firm about these details in advance lets us plan the right vehicle and crew, and avoid nasty surprises on the day. We move people all over the county and well beyond, so unusual access is something we are thoroughly used to handling.

Consider Professional Packing

Long-distance moves put more strain on your belongings, simply because they spend longer on the road. Professional packing reduces the risk of damage and takes an enormous job off your plate at an already busy time. Our full packing service wraps and boxes everything for you, and as a LAPADA member we are well equipped to handle antiques, fine art and high-value pieces with the care they deserve. If you would rather pack the bulk yourself, we are happy to supply quality materials and tackle only the fragile items.

Why Choose a Local Sussex Firm?

When you are moving into an area you may not know well, there is real value in working with a removals company that knows it intimately. We have been moving families and businesses across West Sussex, East Sussex, Surrey, Hampshire and Kent since 2016, and we carry that local knowledge into every job. We know which villages have tricky access, which coastal roads to avoid at certain times, and how to get a loaded vehicle safely down a narrow Downland lane. We are Checkatrade-verified and fully covered, with liability insurance up to £10 million, so your possessions are protected every mile of the journey.

We are also a genuinely family-run business, not a faceless national chain, and we take real pride in looking after people at what is often a stressful and emotional moment. Whether you need a single van and a couple of strong, careful movers or a full house move with packing and storage, we tailor what we do to your move rather than forcing you into a fixed package. You can get a sense of how it all works on our house removals page, and our pricing page explains how everything is costed, with man-and-van services starting from £80.

Settling Into Country and Coastal Life

The move itself is only the beginning. Settling happily into a new community, particularly one with a very different rhythm from the city, takes a little time and a willingness to get involved. The people we move who settle best are invariably those who throw themselves into local life rather than waiting for it to come to them. Sussex villages and seaside towns alike tend to have surprisingly active communities, built around the village hall, the local pub, the church, the sports club, the school gates and the countless societies and groups that thrive outside the cities. Saying yes to the quiz night, joining the walking group or volunteering at a local event is the quickest way to turn neighbours into friends.

It also helps to recalibrate your expectations of pace. The countryside runs on a slower clock, and that is rather the point. Shops may close earlier, trades may take longer to arrive, and the nearest big supermarket may be a drive away. For many city movers this is an adjustment, but it is one most come to treasure, swapping the constant low hum of urban urgency for evenings that feel genuinely their own. Children, in particular, tend to flourish with the space to roam, the proximity to nature and the safety of a smaller community, which is often a decisive factor for the families we help.

Getting to Know the Practicalities

A few practical jobs smooth the transition enormously. Register with a local GP surgery and dentist promptly, as rural practices can have catchment areas and waiting lists. Sort out your broadband and mobile coverage early, ideally before you move, so you are not left without a connection if you work from home. Familiarise yourself with the rubbish and recycling collection days, the location of the nearest recycling centre, and the school catchment boundaries if you have children. None of this is difficult, but doing it in the first week saves a good deal of frustration later.

Timing Your Move Well

One question we are often asked by city movers is when the best time to move actually is. There is no perfect answer, but a few patterns are worth knowing. Summer and the period around the start of the school year tend to be the busiest, as families try to be settled before term begins, which means removal firms book up further ahead and demand is highest. Moving in late autumn, winter or early spring can be quieter and often easier to schedule, though you will want to keep an eye on the weather, as country lanes and exposed coastal roads can be more challenging in poor conditions. Whatever the season, booking your removals firm as early as you can is sensible, especially for a longer-distance move where coordination matters more. We are always happy to talk through timing and to hold a provisional date while your purchase or tenancy falls into place.

Making the Leap

The trend of city exiles heading to the coast and countryside shows no sign of slowing, and it is easy to see why. The space, the scenery, the slower pace and the stronger sense of community answer something a lot of people have been missing. Sussex, with its glorious blend of Downs, Weald and coastline all within reach of London, is about as good a place as any in England to begin that new chapter.

If you are thinking of swapping the city for the Sussex coast or countryside, we would love to help you make the move a smooth one. Call the Wolves Removals team on 01903 893731 or request a free, no-obligation quote through our online quote form, and we will talk through exactly what your move involves. For more advice on planning and settling in, our collection of moving tips is a good place to start, and you might also enjoy our wider look at removals in Sussex and what makes the county such a wonderful place to call home.
faqs: Why are so many people moving from cities to the coast and countryside?

The shift is driven by a combination of factors: the rise of hybrid and remote working has loosened the tie between where people live and where they work, while the desire for more space, better value for money, greener surroundings and a stronger sense of community has grown. Sussex is especially popular because it offers genuine countryside and coastline within easy reach of London.
Which parts of Sussex are most popular with people leaving the city?

Coastal towns such as Worthing and Brighton attract those who want the sea on their doorstep, while inland market towns and Downland villages like Storrington, Horsham and the area around Chichester appeal to people seeking countryside with good amenities and rail links. The right choice depends on whether you prioritise the coast, the countryside or the convenience of a commuter town.
What should I consider before moving from the city to the countryside?

Think about transport, as rural areas have fewer buses and often require a car, and check broadband speeds at the specific property if you work from home. Amenities are more spread out, and coastal homes bring considerations such as salt air and weather exposure. Going in with realistic expectations helps you choose a location that genuinely fits the life you want.
Are long-distance moves out of the city more difficult?

They can involve more logistics, trickier access at the rural end, and longer time on the road, which is why planning matters. Tell your removals firm about parking, narrow lanes, low bridges and stairs at both ends so the right vehicle and crew can be arranged. Professional packing also helps protect belongings on longer journeys.
Can Wolves Removals help me store belongings while I settle in?

Yes. Many people moving to a new area find they have items they want to keep but no immediate room for, so our secure storage solutions can bridge the gap while you settle in and decide where everything belongs. We can combine storage with your removal as part of a single, coordinated move.
